examples.title: Recursive DFS with Edge Classification
examples.description: Complete recursive DFS implementation with timestamps and edge type detection for directed graphs
examples.code:
// Global variables: time = 0
// Colors: 0=WHITE (unvisited), 1=GRAY (in progress), 2=BLACK (finished)

DFS-VISIT(u, graph G):
    time = time + 1
    d[u] = time
    color[u] = GRAY
    
    FOR each v in G.adj[u]:
        IF color[v] == WHITE:
            parent[v] = u
            edgeType[(u,v)] = "TREE_EDGE"
            DFS-VISIT(v, G)
        ELSE IF color[v] == GRAY:
            edgeType[(u,v)] = "BACK_EDGE"
        ELSE:  // color[v] == BLACK
            IF d[u] < d[v]:
                edgeType[(u,v)] = "FORWARD_EDGE"
            ELSE:
                edgeType[(u,v)] = "CROSS_EDGE"
    
    time = time + 1
    f[u] = time
    color[u] = BLACK

DFS(graph G):
    FOR each vertex u in G.V:
        color[u] = WHITE
        parent[u] = NIL
    time = 0
    FOR each vertex u in G.V:
        IF color[u] == WHITE:
            DFS-VISIT(u, G)
examples.explanation: Uses three colors to track vertex state. Tree edge when neighbor is WHITE (unvisited). Back edge when neighbor is GRAY (currently on recursion stack). Forward/cross when neighbor is BLACK (finished). Timestamps d[] and f[] enable interval-based analysis.

examples.title: Iterative DFS with Explicit Stack
examples.description: Stack-based DFS avoiding recursion, useful for large graphs
examples.code:
DFS-ITERATIVE(s, graph G):
    stack = empty
    color[s] = GRAY
    d[s] = ++time
    stack.PUSH(s)
    
    WHILE stack is not empty:
        u = stack.PEEK()
        
        // Find unvisited neighbor
        found = FALSE
        FOR each v in G.adj[u]:
            IF color[v] == WHITE:
                color[v] = GRAY
                d[v] = ++time
                parent[v] = u
                stack.PUSH(v)
                found = TRUE
                BREAK
        
        IF NOT found:
            // All neighbors processed
            f[u] = ++time
            color[u] = BLACK
            stack.POP()
    
    RETURN (d[], f[], parent[])
examples.explanation: Uses explicit stack instead of call stack. Push vertex when discovered, pop when all neighbors processed. Same O(V+E) time but avoids recursion stack overflow. Slight difference: edge classification requires additional tracking since we don't have recursion stack state readily available.

examples.title: Topological Sort using DFS
examples.description: Algorithm to compute topological ordering of a DAG using reverse finish times
examples.code:
TOPOLOGICAL-SORT(graph G):
    // Initialize
    FOR each vertex u in G.V:
        color[u] = WHITE
    
    result = empty list
    time = 0
    
    // DFS to compute finish times
    FOR each vertex u in G.V:
        IF color[u] == WHITE:
            DFS-TS-VISIT(u, G, result)
    
    // result now has vertices in reverse finish order
    RETURN result

DFS-TS-VISIT(u, G, result):
    color[u] = GRAY
    d[u] = ++time
    
    FOR each v in G.adj[u]:
        IF color[v] == WHITE:
            DFS-TS-VISIT(v, G, result)
        // No cycle check here; for cycle detection, detect back edges
    
    color[u] = BLACK
    f[u] = ++time
    // Prepend to result OR append and reverse at end
    result.APPEND(u)  // Add to list (will reverse later)

// To get proper order: reverse(result) before returning
// Or: result.PREPEND(u) to get topological order directly
examples.explanation: Run DFS, record finish times. For DAG, f[u] > f[v] for all edges (u,v). Thus vertices in decreasing f[] order give valid topological sort. Prepend or reverse at end. In graphs with cycles, output is not a valid topological order.
